title: SHIELD Against CCP Act
plain_summary: This law tells the Department of Homeland Security to create a special team to study threats from China's Communist Party. The team will look at how China might harm America through things like cyber attacks, problems at borders and ports, and transportation dangers. The team must write a report every year for seven years telling Congress what they found and what America is doing to stay safe. The government will also research new ways to protect the country from these threats.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Strategic Homeland Intelligence and Enforcement Legislation to Defend Against the CCP Act or the SHIELD Against CCP Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Working Group to Counter Certain Threats Posed to the United States by the Chinese Communist Party
sections.text:
The Secretary of Homeland Security must create a special working group within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed. This working group will focus on stopping terrorist attacks, cyber attacks, border security problems, port security problems, and transportation security threats that come from the Chinese Communist Party.

The working group will have a Director chosen by the Secretary of Homeland Security. The Director will report directly to the Secretary about all the group's work, operations, and security matters. The Secretary must make sure the working group has enough employees to do its job, including at least one person whose only job is to make sure the group follows privacy laws.

The working group can accept workers on temporary assignment from intelligence agencies or other federal agencies. These workers can be experts in stopping threats from the Chinese Communist Party or related areas.

The working group has several main jobs. It must examine, evaluate, and write reports about how the Department of Homeland Security is fighting threats from the Chinese Communist Party. This includes looking at how the Chinese Communist Party uses dishonest tactics and takes advantage of the United States immigration system through identity theft, visa fraud, illegal border crossings, human smuggling, and human trafficking.

The group must also study harmful economic and trade practices by the Chinese Communist Party, including trafficking fake goods, using forced labor, taking advantage of workers for money, customs fraud, and stealing intellectual property and technology.

Another important job is examining how the Chinese Communist Party directly or indirectly supports criminal organizations that traffic fentanyl, illegal drug ingredients, or other controlled substances through the United States border, international mail, or express shipping.

The working group must also look into how the Chinese Communist Party supports illegal financial activities by Chinese Money Laundering Organizations, including sending money back to China or other countries from illegal activities.

The group must keep track of Department of Homeland Security resources used for programs that fight Chinese Communist Party threats and report on how well these programs work. It should build on existing evaluations and avoid duplicating work by reviewing findings from other similar groups or committees.

The working group must identify gaps in the Department's policies, processes, and activities for responding to Chinese Communist Party threats. It should help different offices and parts of the Department work together on a complete response to these threats.

The working group has an additional duty related to sharing information. It must work with the Office of Intelligence and Analysis to review information about Chinese Communist Party threats that comes from federal, state, local, tribal, and territorial partners, as well as from the National Network of Fusion Centers (information sharing centers that help analyze security threats). The group must include this information in its own threat information and make sure this information gets shared back to all these partners.

Starting one hundred eighty days after this law passes and then every year for five years, the Secretary of Homeland Security must submit a report to Congress. This report must assess terrorist, cyber, border security, port security, and transportation security threats from the Chinese Communist Party during the previous twelve months. The Secretary must work with the Under Secretary for Intelligence and Analysis, the Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and the Director of National Intelligence to create this report.

Each yearly report must describe what the working group did and include any other matters the Secretary thinks are important. The report should be unclassified so the public can read it, but it can have a classified section. The Secretary must post the unclassified part on the Department of Homeland Security website. Within thirty days of submitting each report, the Secretary must brief Congress about the report and the working group's progress and challenges.

Within one year of this law passing, the Comptroller General of the United States (the head of the Government Accountability Office) must submit a report to Congress about how well this law is being implemented.

Also within one year, the Secretary of Homeland Security must work with the Director and the Under Secretary for Science and Technology to research and develop new technologies and techniques. These should help the Department improve security and awareness about Chinese Communist Party threats. This can include testing these technologies in real operations when practical.

All activities under this law must follow constitutional protections, privacy laws, civil rights, and civil liberties. The working group cannot interfere with lawful free speech by United States citizens.

The working group will end seven years after it is created.

The law defines several important terms. Appropriate congressional committees means specific committees in the House of Representatives and Senate that deal with homeland security, financial matters, judiciary issues, and foreign affairs. Fusion center means information sharing centers as defined in existing law. Intelligence community means intelligence agencies as defined in the National Security Act. National Network of Fusion Centers means a system of connected fusion centers that share capabilities and expertise to improve security information sharing across the country. United States persons has the same meaning as defined in existing defense authorization law.
